title: Defence against infectious disease
summary: Infectious disease is a contest between pathogens and a layered defence system. This lesson builds the whole of C3.2: what pathogens and infectious disease are; the primary, non-specific defences (skin, mucous membranes, stomach acid and blood clotting); phagocytes and the mechanism of phagocytosis; the specific immune response (lymphocytes, antigens, antibody production by B-cells and plasma cells, and antibody-antigen specificity); immunological memory and the secondary response; the distinctions between active and passive and natural and artificial immunity; how vaccination produces immunity and generates herd immunity; and why antibiotics kill bacteria but not viruses, together with how antibiotic resistance arises. Every worked example and model answer is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks, so you can see how each distinct, valid point earns its credit.
learningObjectives: Define pathogen and infectious disease, and identify the categories of pathogen (bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists).
Describe the primary, non-specific defences — skin as a barrier, mucous membranes, stomach acid and blood clotting to seal wounds — and explain the mechanism of phagocytosis.
Explain the specific immune response: how antigens are recognised, how B-cells and plasma cells produce antibodies, and what antibody-antigen specificity means.
Distinguish active from passive immunity and natural from artificial immunity, and explain how vaccination produces long-term immunity and herd immunity.
Explain why antibiotics act on bacteria and not viruses, and how antibiotic resistance evolves and spreads.
Present answers so that each distinct valid point earns its mark the way our analytic marking engine awards them.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: A Layered Defence
simpleExplanation.summary: Your body meets pathogens with defences arranged in layers. The outer layers block or destroy anything, indiscriminately and immediately. The inner layer is slower but targeted: it learns the identity of a specific invader, mass-produces a matching weapon, and remembers it for next time.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a fortress. The outer wall and its acidic moat (skin, mucous membranes, stomach acid) keep almost everything out, and if the wall is breached a repair crew seals the gap at once (blood clotting). Any invader that slips inside meets patrolling guards who swallow anything foreign on sight (phagocytes) — fast, but they treat every intruder the same. Meanwhile the intelligence service (lymphocytes) studies a captured invader's uniform (its antigens), commissions a weapon shaped to fit only that uniform (antibodies), and keeps a dossier on file (memory cells) so that if the same enemy ever returns, the response is far faster and larger — the enemy is destroyed before it can do harm.
steps: Primary, non-specific defences act first and act on everything: the skin is a physical barrier, mucous membranes trap pathogens, stomach acid destroys swallowed microbes, and blood clotting seals wounds so pathogens cannot enter.
If pathogens get inside, phagocytes engulf and digest them by phagocytosis — still non-specific, still immediate.
The specific immune response is slower but targeted: lymphocytes recognise a pathogen's antigens, and B-cells become plasma cells that secrete antibodies which fit that one antigen.

After the infection clears, memory cells remain. On a second exposure they trigger a faster, larger secondary response, so the pathogen is destroyed before symptoms appear — this is immunological memory, and it is what vaccination exploits.

flashcards.back: An organism or virus that causes disease. The main categories are bacteria, viruses, fungi and protists (protozoa). Not all microorganisms are pathogens.
A disease caused by a pathogen that can be transmitted from one host to another (directly or indirectly). This contrasts with non-infectious conditions such as genetic or deficiency diseases.

Barriers and responses that act against ANY pathogen without prior exposure: skin, mucous membranes, stomach acid and blood clotting, plus phagocytosis. They do not target a particular pathogen and have no memory.
The outer epidermis is a layer of tough, dead, keratinised cells forming a dry, slightly acidic physical barrier that most pathogens cannot penetrate while it is intact.

Moist linings of the airways, gut and other tracts that secrete sticky mucus to trap pathogens; in the airways, cilia sweep the trapped mucus away. Some secretions contain lysozyme, which digests bacterial cell walls.
Hydrochloric acid gives the stomach a very low pH that kills most pathogens swallowed in food, drink or in mucus cleared from the airways — a chemical primary defence.

Platelets release clotting factors that trigger a cascade: prothrombin -> thrombin, which converts soluble fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin. Fibrin forms a mesh that seals the wound, stopping blood loss and sealing the entry route for pathogens.
A non-specific white blood cell (e.g. a macrophage) that engulfs and digests pathogens and debris by phagocytosis. It responds to any foreign material, not to one particular pathogen.
A phagocyte engulfs a pathogen by endocytosis into a vesicle (phagosome); a lysosome fuses with it and digestive enzymes break the pathogen down. Part of the non-specific response.

A molecule (often a surface protein) recognised as foreign that triggers a specific immune response. Each pathogen carries its own antigens — this is what the immune system identifies. Antigen = the marker ON the invader.
A Y-shaped protein (an immunoglobulin) secreted by plasma cells. Its binding site is complementary to ONE specific antigen. Antibody = the weapon MADE BY YOU. Do not confuse it with antigen.

A white blood cell of the specific immune response. B-lymphocytes (B-cells) produce antibodies; each B-cell recognises one antigen. Their targeted action distinguishes them from non-specific phagocytes.
An activated B-cell that has divided and differentiated into an antibody factory, secreting large quantities of one specific antibody during an infection.

An antibody's binding site is complementary in shape to one particular antigen, so it binds only that antigen — like an enzyme and its substrate. One antibody type does not work against a different pathogen.

After an infection, memory cells persist. On re-exposure to the same antigen they trigger a secondary response that is FASTER and LARGER than the primary one, clearing the pathogen before symptoms develop.
Immunity produced when the body's OWN lymphocytes make antibodies and memory cells after meeting an antigen (through infection or vaccination). Long-lasting because memory cells are formed.

Immunity from receiving READY-MADE antibodies from another source (e.g. across the placenta, in breast milk, or by injection). Immediate but temporary — no memory cells are made, so it fades as the antibodies are broken down.

Natural = acquired without medical intervention (infection for active; maternal antibodies for passive). Artificial = acquired through medical intervention (vaccination for active; antibody injection for passive).

Deliberate exposure to antigens from a weakened, killed or fragmented pathogen (or their genetic code) so the immune system mounts a primary response and forms memory cells — active artificial immunity without the disease.
When a high proportion of a population is immune, the pathogen struggles to find susceptible hosts, so transmission slows and even unvaccinated individuals are indirectly protected.

A drug that kills or inhibits BACTERIA by targeting structures or processes unique to them (e.g. cell wall synthesis, bacterial ribosomes). It has no target in viruses, so it does not treat viral infections.

By natural selection, chance-resistant bacteria survive antibiotic treatment while non-resistant ones die; the survivors reproduce, so the resistant allele spreads. Overuse and incomplete courses accelerate this.
ANTIGEN is on the pathogen and triggers the response; ANTIBODY is made by your plasma cells and binds the antigen. Swapping these two words is one of the most common ways to lose a mark.
Antibiotics do NOT work on viruses. Viruses lack the bacterial structures antibiotics target and replicate inside host cells. Writing 'antibiotics kill the virus' scores zero.

quickCheck.prompt: A patient has influenza, a viral infection. Why will a course of antibiotics not cure it?
options: Antibiotics only work if taken before infection
Viruses lack the bacterial structures and processes antibiotics target, so there is nothing for the drug to act on
The dose of antibiotic is always too low for viruses
Antibiotics make viruses reproduce faster
quickCheck.answer: Viruses lack the bacterial structures and processes antibiotics target, so there is nothing for the drug to act on

quickCheck.prompt: Which correctly describes the difference between an antigen and an antibody?
options: An antigen is made by plasma cells; an antibody is on the pathogen
An antigen is a foreign marker that triggers the response; an antibody is a protein made by plasma cells that binds it
They are two names for the same molecule
An antibody is on the pathogen; an antigen is a type of white blood cell
quickCheck.answer: An antigen is a foreign marker that triggers the response; an antibody is a protein made by plasma cells that binds it

quickCheck.prompt: A newborn receives antibodies through its mother's breast milk. This is an example of:
options: Natural active immunity
Artificial active immunity
Natural passive immunity
Artificial passive immunity

quickCheck.prompt: Why is the secondary immune response to a pathogen faster and larger than the primary response?
options: The pathogen is weaker the second time
Memory cells from the first exposure rapidly produce large numbers of the specific antibody
Antibiotics remain in the blood from the first infection
The skin becomes a stronger barrier after infection
quickCheck.answer: Memory cells from the first exposure rapidly produce large numbers of the specific antibody

quickCheck.prompt: A vaccine is injected. It contains weakened pathogen. Which statement best explains why this gives long-term immunity but causes little or no illness?
options: The weakened pathogen provides ready-made antibodies that last for years
The antigens trigger a primary response and the formation of memory cells, but the weakened pathogen cannot cause serious disease
The vaccine kills the pathogen with antibiotics before it multiplies
It provides passive immunity that never fades
quickCheck.answer: The antigens trigger a primary response and the formation of memory cells, but the weakened pathogen cannot cause serious disease

faq.q: What exactly is the difference between an antigen and an antibody?
faq.a: An antigen is a molecule — usually a protein on a pathogen's surface — that your immune system recognises as foreign and that triggers a specific response. An antibody is a protein your own plasma cells make in response, whose binding site is complementary to that antigen. A useful phrasing: the antigen is the marker on the invader; the antibody is the custom weapon your body builds to bind that marker. Because so many mark-scheme points hinge on using the two words correctly, it is worth over-learning the distinction.

faq.q: Why don't antibiotics work against viruses?
faq.a: Antibiotics work by attacking things that only bacteria have — for example bacterial cell wall synthesis or bacterial ribosomes. Viruses have none of these; they are not cells, and they replicate inside the host's own cells using the host's machinery. There is simply nothing for the antibiotic to target, so it leaves the virus untouched. This is why doctors do not prescribe antibiotics for colds or flu, and why doing so anyway is harmful: it does nothing for the illness while helping resistant bacteria to be selected.

faq.q: What is the difference between active and passive immunity?
faq.a: In active immunity your own lymphocytes meet an antigen — through infection or vaccination — and make both antibodies and memory cells; this takes time to develop but lasts a long time because the memory cells persist. In passive immunity you receive ready-made antibodies from someone else (across the placenta, in breast milk, or by injection); protection is immediate but temporary, because no memory cells are made and the borrowed antibodies are gradually broken down. Cross this with natural versus artificial and you get four types: natural active (recovering from an infection), artificial active (vaccination), natural passive (maternal antibodies) and artificial passive (an antibody injection, e.g. after a snakebite).

faq.q: How does vaccinating most of a population protect people who are not vaccinated?
faq.a: This is herd immunity. A pathogen spreads by moving from an infected host to a susceptible one. If a large enough fraction of the population is immune, most of the people an infected individual meets cannot pass the pathogen on, so chains of transmission break and outbreaks fizzle out. As a result, even unvaccinated people — for example newborns or those who cannot be vaccinated for medical reasons — are indirectly protected, because the pathogen rarely reaches them.

faq.q: If my body clears an infection, why can I sometimes catch the 'same' illness again?
faq.a: Immunological memory is specific to particular antigens. If a pathogen changes its surface antigens between exposures — as influenza viruses do frequently — your existing memory cells no longer recognise it, so you mount a fresh primary response and can fall ill again. This is also why the flu vaccine is reformulated most years, whereas diseases whose antigens are stable (such as measles) give lasting immunity after one infection or a full vaccination course.

sections.content: We share the world with vast numbers of microorganisms, and a minority of them are pathogens — organisms or viruses that cause disease. An infectious disease is one a pathogen can pass from host to host, which is what makes defence a matter of survival not just for the individual but for populations. Against this threat the body deploys defences in layers: fast, indiscriminate barriers and cells that act on anything foreign, backed by a slower but precisely targeted response that learns a specific pathogen's identity and remembers it. This lesson works through every layer of C3.2, from skin to antibodies to vaccination, and finishes with why antibiotics defeat bacteria but not viruses — and how misusing them breeds resistance. As with every Paper 2 topic, the worked examples show HOW marks are awarded, because in explanation questions the way you phrase each point decides how many marks you keep.
Pathogens and infectious disease

A pathogen is any organism or virus that causes disease. The major categories are bacteria (e.g. the cause of tuberculosis), viruses (e.g. influenza and HIV), fungi (e.g. athlete's foot) and protists or protozoa (e.g. the malarial parasite). A disease is described as infectious when the pathogen can be transmitted from one host to another — directly, or indirectly through water, food, air or a vector such as a mosquito. This is the feature that separates infectious diseases from non-infectious conditions like genetic disorders or vitamin deficiencies, which cannot be caught. The body's job is to keep pathogens out, and to find and destroy any that get in before they multiply enough to cause harm.
Primary, non-specific defences

The primary defences are called non-specific because they act against any pathogen without needing prior exposure and without singling out a particular invader. The skin is the first physical barrier: its outermost layer is made of tough, dead, keratinised cells, forming a dry surface with a slightly acidic pH that inhibits microbial growth while the skin remains intact. Internal surfaces that cannot be dry and keratinised — the airways, gut and reproductive tract — are lined instead with mucous membranes, which secrete sticky mucus that traps pathogens; in the airways, cilia beat to sweep the trapped mucus up towards the throat, where it is swallowed. Many of these secretions, along with tears and saliva, contain the enzyme lysozyme, which digests bacterial cell walls. Swallowed pathogens then meet a further chemical defence: stomach acid, whose very low pH kills most of them before they can establish an infection.

items: **Skin:** tough, dead keratinised outer cells form a dry, slightly acidic physical barrier.
**Mucous membranes:** secrete mucus that traps pathogens; cilia sweep it away; lysozyme in secretions digests bacterial cell walls.
**Stomach acid:** low pH destroys most swallowed pathogens (a chemical defence).
**Blood clotting:** seals a broken skin barrier, preventing blood loss and closing the entry route for pathogens.
All of these act against ANY pathogen and have no memory — this is what 'non-specific' means.

sections.content: Blood clotting: sealing a breach

When the skin is cut, the barrier is broken and pathogens have a route in, so the body seals the wound quickly by forming a clot. Platelets accumulate at the damaged site and release clotting factors, which set off a cascade of reactions. In outline, the inactive plasma protein prothrombin is converted to the active enzyme thrombin; thrombin then catalyses the conversion of the soluble plasma protein fibrinogen into insoluble fibrin. The fibrin forms a mesh of threads that traps platelets and blood cells, producing a clot that plugs the wound. This stops further blood loss and, crucially for this topic, closes off the entry point so that pathogens cannot get through the broken barrier.

sections.question:
Place the following events of blood clotting in the correct order, then state how clotting contributes to defence against infectious disease.
A. Fibrin forms a mesh that traps blood cells.
B. Thrombin is formed from prothrombin.
C. Platelets release clotting factors at the wound.
D. Fibrinogen is converted to fibrin. [4]
sections.solution:
**Correct sequence: C -> B -> D -> A.** [2 marks: 1 for the correct first-and-last, 1 for the full order]

1. Platelets release clotting factors at the damaged site (C).
2. The cascade produces the active enzyme thrombin from prothrombin (B).
3. Thrombin catalyses conversion of soluble fibrinogen to insoluble fibrin (D).
4. Fibrin forms a mesh that traps blood cells, forming the clot (A).

**Contribution to defence (2 marks):** the clot **seals the wound**, so it stops blood loss AND closes the break in the skin barrier, **preventing pathogens from entering** the body through the cut.

*Note how the marks split: two for the sequence and two for the defence role. A student who only orders the events answers half the question. Read the command terms — 'place in order' AND 'state how' are two separate demands.*

sections.content: Be precise with clotting vocabulary: distinguish fibrinogen (soluble) from fibrin (insoluble), and prothrombin (inactive) from thrombin (active enzyme). Marks are routinely awarded for that exact detail, and for this topic remember to link clotting back to DEFENCE — the sealed wound blocks pathogen entry, not just blood loss.
Phagocytes and phagocytosis

If pathogens breach the primary barriers, phagocytes provide the next non-specific response. Phagocytes are white blood cells — macrophages are the classic example — that patrol the tissues and blood and engulf anything recognised as foreign. The process, phagocytosis, is a form of endocytosis: the phagocyte binds to the pathogen and surrounds it with its membrane, drawing it inside into a vesicle called a phagosome. A lysosome, packed with digestive enzymes, then fuses with the phagosome, and the enzymes break the pathogen down. Because a phagocyte responds to any foreign material in the same way and keeps no record of what it has met, phagocytosis is still part of the non-specific defence — powerful and immediate, but not targeted and without memory.

items: **Recognition and engulfment:** the phagocyte binds the pathogen and engulfs it by endocytosis into a phagosome.
**Fusion:** a lysosome fuses with the phagosome, delivering digestive enzymes.
**Digestion:** the enzymes break the pathogen down into harmless products.
Phagocytes act against any pathogen and keep no memory — non-specific, like the barriers.

sections.question: An electron micrograph shows a macrophage engulfing a bacterium. The scale bar is labelled 1 um and measures 20 mm on the print. The bacterium's diameter measures 10 mm on the print. Calculate the actual diameter of the bacterium in um, showing your working. [3]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 - find the magnification from the scale bar.**
Magnification = image size of scale bar / actual size it represents.
Convert to the same unit: 20 mm = 20 000 um.
Magnification = 20 000 um / 1 um = 20 000x. [M1: correct magnification with consistent units]

**Step 2 - use the magnification to find the actual diameter.**
Actual size = image size / magnification.
Image diameter of the bacterium = 10 mm = 10 000 um. [M1: converts image size and rearranges correctly]
Actual size = 10 000 um / 20 000 = 0.5 um. [A1: 0.5 um]

**Answer: 0.5 um.**

*The whole calculation hinges on converting to one unit before dividing — a classic place to drop a factor of 1000. Show the conversion so that if you slip on the arithmetic, the method marks are still safe (ECF).*

sections.content: The specific immune response: lymphocytes, antigens and antibodies

Pathogens that survive the non-specific defences are dealt with by the specific immune response, carried out by lymphocytes — a class of white blood cell distinct from phagocytes. What makes it 'specific' is that it targets one particular pathogen, recognised by its antigens. An antigen is a molecule, usually a protein on the pathogen's surface, that the immune system identifies as foreign. Each B-lymphocyte (B-cell) carries receptors for one specific antigen. When a B-cell meets its matching antigen it is activated and divides rapidly, and many of its daughter cells differentiate into plasma cells — antibody factories that secrete large quantities of one specific antibody into the blood. An antibody is a Y-shaped protein whose binding site is complementary in shape to that one antigen, so it binds only that antigen and no other. This is antibody-antigen specificity, and it works like the fit between an enzyme and its substrate: one antibody type does not work against a different pathogen. Once bound, antibodies help destroy the pathogen — for example by clumping pathogens together and marking them so that phagocytes engulf them more readily.

items: **Antigen:** a foreign marker (usually a surface protein) on the pathogen that triggers the response — the marker ON the invader.
**Lymphocytes:** white blood cells of the SPECIFIC response; each B-cell recognises one antigen.
**Plasma cells:** activated B-cells that mass-produce and secrete one specific antibody.
**Antibody:** a protein whose binding site is complementary to ONE antigen — the weapon MADE BY YOU.
**Specificity:** an antibody binds only its matching antigen, like an enzyme and its substrate.

sections.content: The single most common error in this topic is swapping 'antigen' and 'antibody'. Lock it in: the ANTIGEN is on the pathogen and triggers the response; the ANTIBODY is made by YOUR plasma cells and binds the antigen. If you write that 'the pathogen makes antibodies' or 'antigens attack the virus', the mark is lost even if the rest of the sentence is right.
Immunological memory and the secondary response

Producing a targeted antibody from scratch takes several days, which is why a first infection can make you ill before it is cleared — the primary response. The lasting benefit comes afterwards. When B-cells are activated, some of their daughter cells do not become plasma cells but persist in the body as memory cells. These carry the receptor for the same antigen and remain in the blood and lymph long after the infection has gone. If the same pathogen is ever encountered again, the memory cells recognise its antigen immediately and divide to form plasma cells very quickly, so antibodies are produced faster and in far greater quantity than before. This secondary response usually destroys the pathogen before it can multiply enough to cause symptoms — so the person is immune. This is immunological memory, and it is the basis of both natural immunity after infection and the immunity produced by vaccination.
Active vs passive, natural vs artificial immunity

Immunity comes in two independent pairs, and combining them gives four categories. Active immunity is produced when your own lymphocytes meet an antigen and make antibodies and memory cells; it develops over time but lasts a long time, because the memory cells persist. Passive immunity comes from receiving ready-made antibodies from another source; it is immediate but temporary, because no memory cells are formed and the borrowed antibodies are gradually broken down. Cutting across this is whether immunity was acquired naturally or artificially. Natural active immunity follows recovery from an actual infection; artificial active immunity follows vaccination. Natural passive immunity is the transfer of a mother's antibodies to her baby across the placenta or in breast milk; artificial passive immunity is an injection of ready-made antibodies, for example the antivenom given after a venomous snakebite when there is no time to build a response.

items: **Active:** your OWN lymphocytes make antibodies and memory cells -> slow to develop, long-lasting.
**Passive:** you RECEIVE ready-made antibodies -> immediate, but temporary (no memory cells).
**Natural active:** immunity after recovering from an infection.
**Artificial active:** immunity from vaccination.
**Natural passive:** maternal antibodies via placenta or breast milk.
**Artificial passive:** injection of ready-made antibodies (e.g. antivenom).

sections.content: Vaccination, immunity and herd immunity

A vaccine works by presenting the immune system with a pathogen's antigens in a form that cannot cause serious disease — a weakened (attenuated) or killed pathogen, a fragment of it such as a surface protein, or genetic instructions for the body to make that antigen itself. Because the antigens are still recognised as foreign, the body mounts a primary response and, crucially, forms memory cells, exactly as it would after a real infection — but without the danger of the disease. If the person later meets the actual pathogen, those memory cells trigger a rapid, large secondary response that destroys it before symptoms appear. This is active artificial immunity. Vaccination also protects the wider population through herd immunity: when a high enough proportion of people are immune, the pathogen can rarely find a susceptible host, so chains of transmission break down and even unvaccinated individuals — such as newborns or people who cannot be vaccinated for medical reasons — are indirectly protected.

When you explain how a vaccine 'produces immunity', make the memory-cell step explicit. Weaker answers stop at 'the vaccine makes antibodies', which does not explain LONG-TERM protection. The mark-earning chain is: antigen in the vaccine -> stimulates specific lymphocytes -> antibodies AND memory cells form -> on later infection the memory cells give a faster, larger secondary response that destroys the pathogen before symptoms.
Antibiotics and antibiotic resistance

Antibiotics are drugs that kill or inhibit bacteria by targeting features unique to bacterial cells — for example the enzymes that build the bacterial cell wall, or bacterial ribosomes, which differ from our own. Because these targets exist only in bacteria, antibiotics can harm the pathogen while leaving human cells unaffected. This same logic explains why antibiotics do NOT work against viruses: a virus is not a cell, has no cell wall or ribosomes of its own, and replicates inside the host's cells using the host's machinery — so there is nothing for the antibiotic to attack. Prescribing antibiotics for a viral illness such as a cold or flu therefore does no good, and it does real harm by promoting resistance.

Antibiotic resistance evolves by natural selection. Within a bacterial population there is variation, and by chance some individuals carry an allele that makes them resistant to a particular antibiotic. When the antibiotic is used, it kills the non-resistant bacteria but the resistant ones survive. These survivors reproduce — and because bacteria reproduce rapidly and can also pass resistance genes to one another — the resistant type comes to dominate the population. Over-prescribing antibiotics and not completing a full course both speed this up: the more often bacteria are exposed to the drug, and the more survivors are left behind by a half-finished course, the stronger the selection for resistance. The result is strains such as MRSA that ordinary antibiotics no longer treat.

items: **Target:** antibiotics act on structures/processes unique to bacteria (e.g. cell wall synthesis, bacterial ribosomes).
**Not viruses:** viruses lack these targets and replicate inside host cells, so antibiotics have nothing to act on.
**Resistance arises by natural selection:** chance-resistant bacteria survive treatment; non-resistant ones die.
**Spread:** survivors reproduce (and can transfer resistance genes), so the resistant allele becomes common.
**Drivers:** overuse and unfinished courses increase the selection pressure for resistance.

sections.question: A hospital notices that an antibiotic which used to cure a bacterial infection is now failing in most patients. Using the theory of natural selection, explain how the bacterial population became resistant to the antibiotic. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
There is genetic **variation** within the bacterial population, and by chance a few bacteria carry an allele that makes them **resistant** to the antibiotic. When the antibiotic is used it kills the non-resistant bacteria, but the **resistant bacteria survive** (the antibiotic acts as a selection pressure). These survivors **reproduce and pass on the resistance allele** to their offspring, and because bacteria reproduce rapidly the proportion of resistant bacteria in the population **increases over successive generations**, until most of the population is resistant and the antibiotic no longer works.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** - each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1:** there is variation in the population / some bacteria are resistant by chance (due to a mutation/allele). [A for the origin of variation]
- **Point 2:** the antibiotic kills non-resistant bacteria but resistant ones survive (antibiotic = selection pressure).
- **Point 3:** the surviving resistant bacteria reproduce / pass on the resistance allele to offspring.
- **Point 4:** the frequency of the resistant type increases over generations, so the population becomes resistant. [A - the conclusion of the reasoning chain]

**ECF and equivalents.** The points are independent, so a candidate who omits, say, Point 1 but gives a clear account of survival, reproduction and increasing frequency still earns those three marks. Equivalent wording is accepted: 'natural selection favours the resistant bacteria' can cover Points 2-3 if the mechanism is clear.

**What the engine rejects.** Answers that say the bacteria 'got used to' or 'wanted to resist' or 'learned to fight' the antibiotic score zero for that idea - resistance is not acquired by intent or exposure; it is selection acting on pre-existing variation.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Saying antibiotics kill viruses** - antibiotics act only on bacteria; viruses lack the targets and replicate inside host cells, so antibiotics do nothing to them.

**Swapping antigen and antibody** - the antigen is on the pathogen and triggers the response; the antibody is made by your plasma cells and binds the antigen. Getting these the wrong way round loses the mark.

**Confusing active and passive immunity** - active means YOUR cells make the antibodies and memory cells (long-lasting); passive means you RECEIVE ready-made antibodies (immediate but temporary, no memory).

**Explaining vaccination without memory cells** - 'the vaccine makes antibodies' does not explain LONG-TERM immunity; you must state that memory cells form and give a faster, larger secondary response on re-infection.
**Calling the specific response 'non-specific'** - phagocytes and barriers are non-specific; lymphocytes, antibodies and memory are the specific response. Keep the two systems distinct.
**Saying bacteria 'try to' or 'learn to' resist antibiotics** - resistance arises by natural selection on pre-existing variation, not by intent or by being 'exposed' and adapting.

**Treating herd immunity as personal protection** - herd immunity protects the population by breaking transmission chains; it is about the proportion immune, not about the vaccine directly protecting each unvaccinated person.

sections.content: Model answer - marked the way our engine marks it

C3.2 explanation questions are marked analytically: each distinct valid biological point is worth one mark. Answer marks (A) credit a correct step in the reasoning, error-carried-forward (ECF) means an earlier slip does not cost you the marks that follow if your method is written down, and equivalent wording is accepted provided the biology is unambiguous. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific, named idea rather than to loose phrasing - and note that hitting the memory-cell step is what turns a partial answer into full marks.

sections.question: Explain how vaccination produces long-term immunity to a specific disease. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
The vaccine contains antigens from the pathogen - for example a weakened, killed or fragmented pathogen - which the immune system recognises as foreign. These antigens stimulate specific lymphocytes (B-cells) to divide, producing plasma cells that secrete antibodies specific to that pathogen. Crucially, memory cells for that antigen are also formed and remain in the body long after the vaccine antigens have gone. If the person is later infected by the actual pathogen, these memory cells recognise its antigen at once and trigger a much faster and larger secondary response, so antibodies are produced quickly enough to destroy the pathogen before it causes symptoms - the person is immune.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** - each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (A):** the vaccine contains ANTIGENS / a weakened (attenuated), killed or fragmented pathogen.
- **Point 2 (A):** these stimulate specific lymphocytes (B-cells) to produce antibodies (via plasma cells).
- **Point 3 (A):** MEMORY CELLS are formed and persist - this is the step that explains LONG-TERM immunity.
- **Point 4 (A):** on later infection, the memory cells give a FASTER and LARGER secondary response that destroys the pathogen before symptoms appear.

**ECF and equivalents.** The points are independent, so a student who does not name plasma cells but correctly states that antibodies and memory cells are made still earns those points. Equivalent wording is accepted: 'attenuated pathogen' for weakened, 'immunological memory' for memory cells, 'secondary response' for the faster/larger reaction. Because there are several creditable ideas here, there is room to miss one and still reach 4.

**What the engine rejects.** An answer that stops at 'the vaccine makes you produce antibodies' scores Points 1-2 at most - it does NOT explain the word LONG-TERM in the question, because it never mentions memory cells or the secondary response. Marks are for distinct ideas: describing antibody production twice in different words scores once.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, name the antigen source, the stimulation of specific lymphocytes, the FORMATION OF MEMORY CELLS, and the faster/larger secondary response on re-infection. The memory-cell step is the one weak answers omit - and it is the one the word 'long-term' is testing.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way - point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name the antigen in the vaccine, the stimulation of specific lymphocytes, the FORMATION OF MEMORY CELLS, and the faster/larger secondary response. Vague phrases like 'it builds up your immune system' earn nothing; the mark scheme rewards the named biological steps, and showing each one is what protects your score.
Where this leads

The ideas in C3.2 connect widely across the course. Antibody-antigen specificity is the molecular-recognition principle you meet again in enzyme-substrate binding and receptor signalling; immunological memory is the reason vaccination has controlled diseases that once killed millions; and antibiotic resistance is natural selection observed in real time, linking this topic straight to evolution and to one of the most pressing problems in modern medicine. Master the habit of writing immune explanations as a chain of distinct, named steps - antigen recognised, specific lymphocytes activated, antibodies and memory cells made, secondary response on re-exposure - and you have a template that answers almost any question this topic can ask.
